name: Nitrogen
name_origin: From Greek 'nitron' (native soda) and 'genes' (forming), meaning 'soda-forming'; coined in French as 'nitrogene' by Jean-Antoine Chaptal in 1790.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Nitrogen as N2 makes up ~78% of Earth's atmosphere by volume, the largest accessible reservoir of any element. It is also fixed into nitrates, ammonia, amino acids, and nucleic acids, where it is essential to all known life.
states: in proteins, nucleic acids, and other biomolecules
ammonia and ammonium in soils and water

compounds.name: Ammonia
compounds.summary: A pungent base produced industrially via the Haber-Bosch process and used as the foundation of nitrogen fertilizers.

compounds.name: Nitric acid
compounds.summary: A strong oxidizing acid used in fertilizer, explosive, and metallurgical industries.

compounds.name: Nitrous oxide
compounds.summary: A mild anesthetic and dental analgesic ('laughing gas'); also a propellant in aerosol whipped cream.

compounds.name: Nitrogen dioxide
compounds.summary: A red-brown toxic gas, a major air pollutant from combustion that contributes to smog and acid rain.

compounds.name: Sodium nitrate
compounds.summary: Known as Chile saltpeter, used historically in fertilizer, explosives, and as a food preservative.

compounds.name: Nitroglycerin
compounds.summary: An unstable nitrate ester used both as a powerful explosive (in dynamite) and a vasodilator drug for angina.

uses: Synthesis of ammonia for fertilizer (Haber-Bosch process)
Inert atmosphere for food packaging, electronics manufacturing, and chemical processes
Cryogenic coolant (liquid nitrogen) in biological storage and food freezing
Manufacture of nitric acid, explosives (TNT, ammonium nitrate), and propellants
Tire inflation in aircraft and racing cars (low moisture, stable pressure)
Pressurizing fluid in oil and gas wells and fire-suppression systems
reactivity_summary: Diatomic N2 is exceptionally inert because of its very strong triple bond (945 kJ/mol), making industrial 'fixation' of atmospheric nitrogen energy-intensive. Once fixed, nitrogen forms a very wide range of oxides, hydrides, and organonitrogen compounds, and many nitrogen-rich molecules (azides, nitro compounds) are powerful explosives.
isotopes_summary: Nitrogen has two stable isotopes: 14N (~99.6%) and 15N (~0.4%). 15N is used as a non-radioactive tracer in biological and ecological studies; the radioisotope 13N (half-life 10 minutes) is used in PET imaging.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Nitrogen
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
